data.title: Is the end of SaaS coming? The bullish logic behind software stocks.
data.description:
Author: Anthony J. Pompliano, Founder and CEO of Professional Capital Management; Translated by: Shaw & Jinse Finance

The term "SaaS doomsday theory" has quickly become popular on Wall Street. Since last September, the S&P North American Technology Software Index has lost 32% of its market capitalization. Such a huge drop in just a few months is extremely rare.

Salesforce's stock price has fallen by more than 26%, Adobe's market capitalization has shrunk by 20%, and the stock prices of dozens of mid-cap SaaS companies have been halved. If you have been following enterprise software stocks recently, you will have noticed that the entire sector is undergoing a complete revaluation. For investors holding these stocks, the performance of their holdings is undoubtedly worrying.

But what if everyone panics and prepares to cut their losses and exit at the bottom of the pullback? What if the worst is over? I don't have a crystal ball to predict the future, nor do I intend to accurately predict the market bottom, but contrarian investors would argue that the market is overreacting, the mainstream bearish logic contradicts reality, and investors entering the market now may be facing the best opportunity to position themselves once in a decade. Before discussing future trends, let's clarify why the market has reached this point. This round of decline was caused by two distinct shocks. The first was the impact of the macroeconomy. In 2022, the Federal Reserve raised interest rates at the fastest pace in history, severely impacting duration assets. Few assets have a duration exceeding that of high-growth software companies—their cash flow valuations often project a decade into the future. Taking the Bethesda Cloud Index as an example, the median revenue valuation of listed SaaS companies plummeted from a historical peak of 18.4 times forward revenue in September 2021 to approximately 6 times in early 2026. This adjustment alone was extremely drastic. This valuation metric alone has plunged by 65%. The second wave of impact comes from structural changes in the industry. Frankly, this is what has caused even greater panic among investors. The core reason can be summarized as follows: the rise of AI intelligent agents has led to what analysts call seat compression—that is, a single AI agent can perform the work of multiple employees, thus reducing the number of software licenses required by enterprises. After Atlassian first announced a decline in the number of corporate seats, its stock price plummeted by 35%. Workday announced 8.5% layoffs, directly citing AI as one of the reasons. If these examples aren't alarming enough, a CIO survey in January 2026 shows that enterprise IT budget growth is projected to be only 3.4%. Most analysts believe this significant slowdown in budget growth is due to funds being diverted to AI infrastructure construction by hyperscale cloud vendors, with planned spending exceeding $660 billion. This figure is undoubtedly staggering, and once the market sees such massive funding coupled with panic selling, it quickly triggers a sell-off. This was precisely the market trend at the beginning of this year. A typical manifestation was that enterprise price-to-sales ratios compressed from 9 times to about 6 times within weeks, a level that is the lowest since the mid-2010s. However, it's worth noting that the decline in software stocks is not uniform. A clear divergence has emerged between AI infrastructure software and traditional application software. Palantir's stock price surged 135% in 2025 due to a 121% year-over-year increase in US commercial revenue, while also providing a revenue guidance of $7.2 billion for fiscal year 2026, significantly exceeding analysts' expectations. High-quality companies with genuine growth can almost defy market conditions and navigate any cycle. Similar examples include: Microsoft Azure's quarterly revenue exceeding $50 billion, with a year-over-year growth rate of 39%; Oracle Cloud Infrastructure's quarterly growth of 84%, and the announcement of a $553 billion backlog of orders. A company with a backlog of nearly a trillion dollars is hard not to consider more valuable in the future. Now let's compare this to the other end of the market. Salesforce is a lamentable example, with its market value evaporating by more than a quarter; Adobe's annual revenue growth rate is still 12%, but its forward P/E ratio has been compressed to about 10—such a low valuation usually means the company has entered a permanent decline. The situation with CrowdStrike is even more perplexing: as a recognized structural winner in the cybersecurity field, its market position is becoming increasingly important with the proliferation of AI, yet its valuation is 20% lower than the five-year average price-to-sales ratio. From these cases, I draw the core conclusion that the market is treating all non-infrastructure software as a liability. Contrarian investors believe this is precisely the market's mistake. So where exactly is the problem? The mainstream bearish view holds that AI will disrupt the SaaS industry. Pessimists claim that enterprises (especially large corporations) will stop paying for software subscriptions because AI agents will handle a large amount of work. Is this possible? Of course. But based on the current facts, it's by no means an obvious conclusion. Contrarian investors might point out a fatal flaw in this logic: Industry leaders aren't sitting idly by; they're building their own AI layers, leveraging their two decades of accumulated exclusive enterprise data, customer relationships, and distribution channels to create advantages that startups can't replicate in a short time. In short, traditional giants are only disrupted if they don't proactively innovate. Take Salesforce as an example: its Agentforce AI platform achieved $800 million in annual recurring revenue in fiscal year 2026, a surge of 169% year-over-year, and is currently transitioning to a pay-as-you-go, results-based pricing model. If this model scales successfully, startups will find it difficult to compete with Salesforce's channel advantages. Another noteworthy example is ServiceNow's generative AI suite, Now Assist, whose annual contract value has already exceeded $600 million and is expected to reach $1 billion by the end of the year. A $1 billion contract is a significant sum. Not to mention software industry giant Microsoft. It launched a new Microsoft 365 Enterprise plan, priced at $99 per user per month, 65% higher than its previous highest plan, aiming to directly monetize AI value through existing users. Therefore, despite the clamor of bearish voices, these cases do not threaten the SaaS business model; rather, they signify that the SaaS model is evolving into a more powerful form, directly benefiting from the AI ​​technology wave. Another key point is that many companies are shifting from a per-user licensing model to a usage-based billing model—no longer charging per person, but rather based on completed tasks or delivered results. This business model iteration, coupled with the expected 15% growth in enterprise software spending this year, makes the pessimistic arguments about software stocks increasingly untenable. The companies best positioned to absorb this incremental spending are precisely the industry leaders who already possess strong enterprise customer relationships, compliance systems, and workflow integration capabilities. Bain & Company's research also confirms that the vast majority of customers prefer to purchase AI-enabled solutions from existing suppliers. This makes sense: as long as the technology and cost are competitive, companies will prioritize familiar partners and trusted brands. So, what conditions need to be met to discredit the bears and restore confidence in software stocks? This situation is slightly more complex, and there is no easy solution. The market needs several key factors to change. First, AI monetization must break through a credibility threshold: if Agentforce's annual recurring revenue exceeds $1 billion, market confidence in Salesforce will recover; ServiceNow's Now Assist needs to achieve $1 billion in annual contract value; and Microsoft needs to demonstrate that Copilot can consistently and quantifiably increase average revenue per user. These data are likely to be released as early as the second half of this year, at which point the market narrative will shift from "AI disrupting SaaS" to "SaaS monetizing through AI." Secondly, IT budget data from multiple companies needs to be verified. Even with a reduction in available seats, software spending still saw net growth. This is the most concrete evidence to refute the bearish logic. Finally, a stable macroeconomic environment is crucial. If interest rate hikes continue, the sector will continue to be under pressure; however, if interest rates remain unchanged or even begin to cut, the pressure of valuation compression will gradually dissipate, and a genuine upward revaluation will begin. If the bears are wrong, how much can investors gain in the future? The good news is that the valuations of high-quality software stocks are already at their most attractive levels in years. Microsoft's forward P/E ratio is approximately 24, with an expected annualized earnings growth of 14%. The average Wall Street target price is $600, representing about a 50% upside from the current share price. Cloudflare is similar, with an average analyst target price of $245, representing about a 40% upside from the current price. Snowflake's revenue growth is still at 29% year-over-year, its P/S ratio is 13, and consensus estimates indicate a 43% upside. These upside potentials are substantial and do not yet include the most optimistic analyst targets. For those seeking extreme asymmetric returns, contrarian investors would consider Adobe the best option. Its current forward P/E ratio is approximately 10, and its revenue and profit are still maintaining double-digit growth. As mentioned earlier, the market has priced it in as a company in structural recession, while it continues to grow. If Adobe's valuation returns to a normal P/E ratio of 25, the valuation repair alone would represent a gain of approximately 150%, not even factoring in earnings growth. In the US stock market, such cases abound. Multiple analysts predict that high-quality software stocks that successfully monetize AI could achieve a 40%-50% increase simply through valuation repair, not even considering the earnings growth brought about by technological innovation. It's important to remember that the market's reaction to the "SaaS doomsday" has been excessive, even believing that the entire industry will be disrupted to the point of insignificance. However, the data tells a completely different story: Perhaps SaaS companies are not being disrupted by external forces, but rather evolving internally into a more valuable form—higher revenue per customer, lower marginal delivery costs, and a potential market size that will double in the next four years. If this is indeed the case, the recent investor panic has already resulted in a "limited-time discount" on the entry price for these companies. The key question is, in hindsight, whether this panic was truly justified.

data.title: The Wall Street Journal exposes the inside story of Sora's shutdown: Is the OpenAI bubble about to burst?
data.description:
On March 24th, Sam Altman sent an email to internal employees announcing the shutdown of Sora. There was no grand farewell, no roadmap, no "we'll be back with a better product." Only one sentence: We need to use computing power for more important things.

Then the Wall Street Journal published an exclusive big news, exposing the inside story of Sora's shutdown~

Sora's direct cause of death was its excessively high costs. Sora burned through approximately one million dollars in computing power costs every day. Its peak user base was less than one million, and it continued to decline thereafter, with fewer than 500,000 active users before its shutdown.

The total revenue throughout the product's lifecycle was only two million one hundred thousand dollars.

While Disney had committed a $1 billion investment to the partnership, not only providing financial support but also licensing Sora to use IPs such as Mickey Mouse, Iron Man, and Darth Vader.

The direct cause of Sora's shutdown and the partnership blunder

Sora's direct cause of death was its excessively high costs. Sora burned through approximately one million dollars in computing power costs every day. Its peak user base was less than one million, and it continued to decline, with active users falling short of five hundred thousand before its shutdown.

The total revenue throughout the product's lifecycle was only two million one hundred thousand dollars.

Disney had committed a $1 billion investment to the partnership, providing not only financial support but also licensing Sora to use IPs such as Mickey Mouse, Iron Man, and Darth Vader ...>

No wonder Disney is so excited about Seedance's development...

OpenAI executives once likened this deal to "the end of the silent film era with sound films."

OpenAI executives once compared this deal to "sound films ending the silent film era."

However, Disney only learned of Sora's impending shutdown less than an hour before the announcement, a rather embarrassing situation... OpenAI has lost all its technological and strategic advantages. While Sora was shut down, ByteDance's Seedance was thriving, with independent evaluations showing Seedance leading across multiple performance metrics, including cue word adherence and multi-camera consistency. OpenAI not only lost to its competitors in video technology but was also strategically crushed by rivals. While the entire OpenAI team was still struggling with Sora, Claude Code was stealing software engineers and enterprise clients. OpenAI once possessed a special power: whatever it released defined the boundaries of possibility for the industry. The Bubble Structure of the Largest Private Equity Funding Round in History In the same month that Sora shut down, OpenAI completed a funding round. $110 billion in size, valued at $840 billion, the largest private equity funding round in history, bar none.

Moreover, the structure of this financing itself reflects a huge bubble:

  • Amazon committed $50 billion, but only $15 billion is immediate cash, with the remaining $35 billion subject to conditions—according to sources, the conditions may include OpenAI achieving specific AI capability milestones or completing an IPO by the end of 2026. Nvidia's $30 billion is primarily a GPU computing power commitment, not a cash equity investment. In other words, this money is essentially a "pre-purchase of GPUs." SoftBank's $30 billion was initially planned to be raised through bridge loans and financing from major financial institutions. To raise funds, SoftBank has begun selling existing holdings, including Nvidia. Therefore, as of the end of March, OpenAI had only received $25 billion in cash: $15 billion from Amazon + $10 billion from SoftBank's first installment. AI Bubble Could Collapse US Stocks Currently, SpaceX (which merged with XAI), OpenAI, and Anthropic, the three major AI giants, are all preparing for their IPOs this year, and their valuations all exceed one trillion US dollars! Before the internet was supposed to change the world, it first trapped Nasdaq investors for over a decade. Amazon's stock price plummeted from $107 to $6, a 94% drop; Microsoft's stock didn't recover its 1999 high until 2016. Wouldn't it be fair to say that this AI bubble will trap investors for another ten or eight years?

data.title: Artemis: A 2030 Outlook for the Machine Economy
data.description:
Author: Lucas Shin, Source: Artemis, Translated by: Shaw & Jinse Finance

Summary

  • By 2030, intelligent agents (AI agents) will become the primary way people use the internet.

  • The new agent-based networks require corresponding new payment channels, monetary systems, and basic components.

  • Value will be concentrated in three layers: the entry layer, the main entity controlling user interaction; the payment layer, the main entity intervening in the flow of funds; and the computing power and hosting layer, the main entity operating the infrastructure. Long-tail intelligent agent business activities will operate based on open protocols. Let's first depict a scenario. The year is 2030. You are 24 years old, living in Burlington, Vermont, and passionate about investing—primarily in US stocks, and also participating in some cryptocurrency and prediction market trading on Kalshi. Two months ago, you started a fintech consulting firm part-time. Some days, like today, start very suddenly. **Buzz—** Your phone rings out, like a bucket of cold water splashed on your face. It's a message from your personal intelligent agent, Nexus: **Good morning, Joe.** I accomplished the following overnight: **Portfolio Update:** Reduced my $WMT holdings by 15% overnight. Satellite data shows declining store traffic, and earnings sentiment has turned bearish, which has been cross-validated. **Schedule Update:** Three meetings are scheduled for this afternoon, and presentations have been pinned to the meeting notes. **Spending Optimization:** Found a new cloud server provider—comparable performance, with the annual fee reduced from $840 to $290. Migration is possible at any time.

    Total Cost: $0.67

    What really happens while you sleep?

    1. Nexus sent a research sub-agent, costing $0.24, to retrieve information from 40 different data providers overnight. It compared Walmart's latest earnings call with satellite imagery of parking lots across U.S. stores, updating your investment logic. When satellite data showed a decline in Walmart's customer traffic, your portfolio agent compared it to Kalshi's earnings sentiment market, confirmed a bearish signal, and reduced its holdings before you woke up. Four years ago, this type of trading strategy was the domain of Citadel and a few quantitative funds, who paid millions of dollars for satellite imagery subscriptions.

      1. Even a Bloomberg terminal costing $30,000 a year can't cover all the information—you still need to subscribe to satellite imagery, alternative data, and spend hours integrating and analyzing it. Now, a 24-year-old in Vermont can gain the same informational advantage as a quantitative analyst at Citadel Securities for less than the cost of a cup of coffee. Nexus's sales sub-agent filters out 200 leads that match your target customer profile—fintech companies in the southeastern United States that are Series B or later and haven't yet used data service providers—and completes the information for $0.002 per lead, using an API developed by another agent and listed on the open market. It then selects the three most interested leads and contacts their scheduling agents to negotiate meeting times. Before each meeting, it retrieves the potential client's alma mater, mutual contacts, company news, and funding history, and prepares a one-page summary for you, pinned to your meeting notes. **For lead information completion alone, a SaaS subscription would cost $200 per account per month.** Nexus's operations sub-agent compared your consulting website with six server providers: Vercel, Render, Railway, Fly.io, Netlify, and Cloudflare. It used trial APIs from each provider at a very low cost to deploy test environments and measure latency, availability, and throughput. Ultimately, Railway achieved equivalent performance at one-third the cost. Nexus negotiated a monthly fee with Railway's pricing agent, built a website mirror on a new server, and completed a full suite of tests to ensure proper operation. **Without an agent, this would have taken at least a week: online research, price negotiations, and the stressful manual migration.** You simply need to confirm with Nexus. Your agent accomplished all of this for just $0.67. Now, multiply this scenario by every knowledge worker, every company, and every operating intelligent agent globally. Buzz— Nexus: Insufficient balance, $1.87 remaining. You, like last week, top up $5 via your Apple Pay-linked credit card and continue brushing your teeth. Underlyingly, this $5 is converted from the credit card into stablecoins—but you see no wallet, don't consider depositing funds, and have no contact with the blockchain. This is a glimpse into the machine economy—a completely new business scenario where AI agents continuously spend money on things humans have never paid for before, with transaction volume and speed far exceeding the scope of human commerce. Imagine billions of transactions generated daily. But today's internet is not yet ready to support all of this. The current internet is designed for humans. It filters non-human behavior through rate limiting, CAPTCHAs, and API keys, and monetizes with human users through advertising. However, with the proliferation of autonomous proxies, this business model will become completely ineffective. Traffic will surge, but effective attention will plummet. Web servers that have long relied on advertising revenue for subsidies will face an order-of-magnitude increase in requests, and these requests will never be affected by advertising. Proxy payments naturally solve this problem; small payments will become the key to access. Paid crawling, paid access, paid use. Companies that build the infrastructure that will eventually be widely adopted by agents will capture the largest pool of new economic activity we will see in our generation. Existing giants are already scrambling to secure their positions, but the machine economy will also give birth to its own new giants. The last wave of the new internet gave birth to Google, Amazon, Facebook, PayPal, and Salesforce. The era of the intelligent agent-based internet is coming. Market Size Outlook By 2030, the vast majority of online interactions will no longer be completed through browsers. Our intelligent agents will browse, test, negotiate, form sub-agent teams, and execute transactions. Each task they complete will generate a series of small payments. These per-use costs may seem like additional expenses, but they are actually replacing much more expensive tools and manpower. The more advanced the available tools, the better the agents will perform, and we will give them greater autonomy. Demand and Speed ​​of Adoption Let's make a rough estimate. In the previous example, Joe's agent completed hundreds of transactions for only $0.67. If we scale this up to a medium-sized enterprise of 500 people—each employee equipped with a personal agent, plus hundreds of shared agents in departments such as sales, finance, legal, and operations—it's easy to generate 100,000 agent-initiated transactions every day. There are over 1 billion knowledge workers globally, 88% of whom already use AI in their work, indicating a huge and continuously growing demand. However, currently, such uses are mostly limited to basic tasks, such as web searches, document summarization, or email composition. The full-scale shift to intelligent agents has not yet arrived, but once it begins, the speed will be extremely fast. Instagram reached 100 million users in 30 months, TikTok in 9 months, and ChatGPT in just 2 months (Reuters/UBS data). One reason for ChatGPT's rapid adoption is that its chat interface was already familiar, requiring no learning of new software or changes in usage habits—you simply describe your needs, and the agent will handle the rest. The only obstacle is trust, and trust has been built far faster than expected. Currently, Claude Code contributes 4% of all public code commits on GitHub (over 135,000 per day), and at the current growth rate, it is projected to exceed 20% by the end of 2026. This represents a 42,896-fold increase in just 13 months. Developers have gone from skepticism to entrusting large-scale production-grade code to AI in just over a year. As models become more intelligent, interfaces become simpler, and more technical complexity is abstracted and hidden, I believe the adoption of intelligent agents will accelerate further. By 2030, even if only 60% of knowledge workers use agents, spending an average of $3 to $5 per day (this is a conservative estimate—Joe only spent $0.67 to complete three tasks before breakfast), the transaction volume of agent transactions on a personal level alone will reach $800 billion to $1.4 trillion annually. In his article, Dragonfly's Robbie Peterson points out that commercial intelligent agents represent a logical evolution of the SaaS model. I wholeheartedly agree. They will no longer merely assist workflows, but will completely replace them. Just as over 95% of software spending today comes from enterprises and government agencies, the usage and spending on intelligent agents in the enterprise sector is likely to far exceed that in the consumer market. We are already witnessing this transformation. Klarna saved approximately $2 million by replacing Salesforce with an internal AI system. ZoomInfo built an AI agent to replace its transaction approval department, saving over $1 million annually. These are just early examples of how individual workflows can be delegated, resulting in millions in cost savings. Every company has hundreds of such processes in sales, finance, legal, operations, and R&D departments. Once intelligent agents are deployed company-wide, the associated expenditures will be staggering.

        Everyone can be a merchant

        As code-based agents drastically reduce development costs, the barrier to entry for online merchants is approaching zero. A wedding planner skilled in venue selection can package and sell optimal workflows. An independent developer in Lagos can develop a vertical API and start earning revenue from agents worldwide within hours. All you need is expertise and to generate an API using prompts to start receiving payments.

        But what happens when an agent starts selling services to other agents?

        Let's assume Joe, mentioned earlier, wants to enter a new field: mid-sized healthcare companies in the American Midwest with outdated payment infrastructure. If his agent completes the reasoning from scratch, the token cost will accumulate rapidly: Screening 200 companies that match a specific profile (reasoning + API calls): Approximately 500,000 tokens. Refining each lead's information (technology stack, funding, recruitment data): 200 leads × approximately 5,000 tokens = 1 million tokens. Locking in the decision-makers of core clients: Approximately 200,000 tokens. Scoring based on intent signals (recruitment pace, contract cycle): Approximately 300,000 tokens. Researching the background of each decision-maker: 20 leads × approximately 10,000 tokens = 200,000 tokens. Writing personalized outreach copy: 20 leads × approximately 3000 tokens = 60,000 tokens. This totals approximately 2.3 million tokens. Using cutting-edge models like Opus 4.6, the cost is between $8 and $15. Wait a minute, didn't Joe's sales sub-agents only spend a few cents on a similar process before? That's right. Because most steps have already been handled by other agents. Lead completion, intent scoring, and scheduling are all packaged interfaces on the open market, costing only a fraction of a cent in a dollar. This model creates entirely new business scenarios. The market supply will grow in both directions: humans build services, and agents are also building services. The high token consumption problem solved by one agent can become a cheap tool that all subsequent agents can use. In such a world, agents can distill their experience into workflows and sell them to other agents to offset their operating costs. Every paradigm shift gives rise to new merchants. Shopify empowers e-commerce sellers, Stripe empowers online businesses, and the machine economy will empower improvisational developers and autonomous intelligent agents. Reality Review: So, how far are we from truly commercialized intelligent agent transactions? My team at Artemis has been tracking the progress of two major agent payment protocols: Coinbase's open-source x402 protocol and the Machine Payment Protocol (MPP) jointly launched by Stripe and Tempo. In short, both types of protocols share the same goal: to allow users or agents to pay for any network service (such as data, web scraping, model inference, or other API services) with a single network request, eliminating the cumbersome processes of account registration, API keys, and billing. Currently, it is still in its early stages. The x402 protocol's transaction volume at the end of 2025 was inflated due to meme coin speculation and leaderboard manipulation. The above chart shows the "real" transaction activity adjusted after filtering out fake transactions using a proprietary algorithm. After filtering out the noise from fake transactions and meme speculation, it's clear that the proxy economy hasn't truly arrived. Most current activity is simply developers testing paid APIs and AI tools, not a genuine proxy economy in operation. Before this model truly takes off, two core issues urgently need to be addressed: The supply side is not yet fully formed: the number of practical APIs that can generate genuine willingness among proxies to pay is severely insufficient. There is a lack of a mature discovery and aggregation layer: even if high-value interfaces exist, proxies currently lack reliable ways to discover them. Since the entire ecosystem is still developing, using transaction volume as the core metric is premature. A more reasonable indicator is the growth of the supply side, namely the number of merchants providing services to proxies. We collectively refer to these merchants as service providers. The image above shows the cumulative change in the number of eligible service providers (sellers) over time. Eligible sellers must meet the following criteria: complete at least two “genuine” transactions and have at least two independent buyers. Last October, this number was less than 100, but now it exceeds 4,000. I expect this growth rate to accelerate, primarily driven by three trends: Artificial intelligence is lowering the barrier to entry for creating digital products (as mentioned earlier), meaning more people and AI agents will become merchants. New services will be designed with agents in mind. Agents are becoming core customers, and the product forms built for them will be drastically different: APIs will replace web pages, instant access will replace registration processes, and pay-as-you-go will replace subscription models. Existing service providers will be forced to transform. As more users interact through AI interfaces rather than manually browsing web pages, advertising-dependent business models will become completely ineffective because there will be no human user attention to monetize. Businesses will have no choice but to charge directly for content and services. These forces will form a positive flywheel, amplifying supply and demand, ultimately igniting the entire agency economy. The agency transaction ecosystem is rapidly taking shape. Numerous startups are emerging, focusing on addressing every gap in this architecture; simultaneously, growth-stage companies in the fintech and software services (SaaS) sectors are also transitioning to native agency transactions. In the past twelve months, almost all major payment giants and AI labs have launched or announced agency transaction-related agreements. We have identified over 170 companies covering five tiers: user interface, intelligent agent, account system, payment infrastructure, and AI engine. This section simplifies to approximately 80 core organizations:

        We break it down layer by layer from top to bottom.

        Interface Layer

        The interface layer is closest to the user and is responsible for directing user intent (demand) to the required tools or services (supply). Whoever defines how intelligent agents discover, evaluate, and select services has enormous control over all the layers below.

        We will focus on the two most important categories in this tier:

        User Interface

        This is the entry point for most people to directly interact with intelligent agents. Apple, Google, OpenAI, Anthropic, xAI, and Perplexity are all developing these types of interfaces, and their forms are rapidly moving beyond simple chat modes. New forms such as voice assistants, desktop assistants, embedded co-pilots, and browser agents are constantly emerging, closely matching users' real-world usage scenarios. The platform that becomes the user's default AI interface will become the starting point for all transactions initiated by the agent, and the winner in this track will gain a significant additional advantage. AI labs have already crawled and trained data from the entire internet; the best remaining training data is now human guidance and feedback. Every time you accept or reject a reply, make a correction, or provide preference information to Claude or ChatGPT, the interface you use captures this data for sales or model training. Controlling the interface means controlling the feedback loop that optimizes the user experience and the model itself. This is why Anthropic launched Claude Code, Google acquired Windsurf, and OpenAI attempted to acquire Cursor. Once your agent accumulates contextual information about your preferences, workflows, and frequently used tools, the user's migration costs become extremely high.

        Service Discovery

        When Joe's agent needs clues to complete an interface or satellite data service provider, how can it find the right service? This is perhaps the biggest unsolved problem in the entire ecosystem architecture. Current solutions are mostly hard-coded tool lists or curated service marketplaces. Major platforms are already building their own systems: OpenAI and Stripe launched ACP, Google and Shopify launched UCP, and Visa launched TAP. These are essentially merchant directories, requiring active integration from both the platform and the merchant to be effective.

        Intelligent Agents and Account Layer

        To accomplish tasks for us, intelligent agents need more than just intelligence. Joe's sales sub-agent completed the entire process of screening 200 leads, completing information, and scheduling three meetings, all without Joe needing to configure any tools, manage API keys, or approve each step individually. Most of the infrastructure supporting all of this is invisible to the end user, but without this infrastructure, the agent is merely a large, incapable language model.

        Here is an overview of the core foundational components needed to achieve all of this:

        Tools and Standards

        These protocols and frameworks empower intelligent agents to interact with the outside world. MCP (Machine Communication Protocol, initiated by Anthropic and now managed by the Linux Foundation) allows agents to interface with external data and tools: calling APIs they've never encountered before, reading databases, or instantly invoking services. A2A (proposed by Google) defines how agents developed on different platforms can discover each other and collaborate.

        Identity Authentication

        After agents can communicate, trust needs to be established. Before conducting transactions or selling services, agents must prove their authorized entity and operating authority, and retain records of their actions that can be verified by other agents.

        Currently, there are various technical approaches, including: biometric authentication (Worldcoin, Civic), on-chain agent reputation systems (ERC-8004), and verifiable credentials (Dock, Reclaim).

        This area offers vast design possibilities but is also extremely risky: What is the maximum amount your agent can spend before obtaining your approval? Can it sign contracts on your behalf? Can authority be delegated to sub-agents? These rules and security boundaries will most likely be ultimately determined at the account level. Clearly, agents must have a wallet to make payments. Coinbase, Safe, MetaMask, Phantom, MoonPay, Privy, and many other vendors are all investing in this area, offering features such as programmatic access and creation, permission delegation, single transaction spending limits, whitelisting of recipients, and cross-chain operation capabilities, eliminating the need for users to manually confirm each operation. This is one of the most competitive tracks in the entire ecosystem, which raises a crucial question: where exactly do these companies' competitive advantages lie? Will this field eventually become homogenized? The payment layer is located deep within the overall architecture and should be imperceptible to end users, but every transaction in the machine economy flows through it. When Joe's agent pays $0.24 at night to retrieve data from 40 service providers, he doesn't need to select a card organization, currency, or settlement public chain for each transaction. The core challenge lies in the fact that traditional payment channels are designed for humans clicking a "buy" button, not for handling the thousands of API calls made by agents per minute, with each transaction amounting to less than a cent. Bank card networks incur a fixed cost of approximately $0.03–$0.04 per transaction, plus a 2.3%–2.9% transaction fee. This is feasible for a $400 hotel booking, but completely unsuitable for the new multi-step agent transactions. This has spurred the development of entirely new protocols and monetary systems specifically designed for agent transactions, while traditional giants are also modifying their existing infrastructure to accommodate these demands. The core points are as follows: These protocols and standards define how smart agents initiate, route, and complete payment settlements. Currently, two main technical approaches have emerged: x402 (Coinbase/Cloudflare) and MPP (Stripe/Tempo) are designed specifically for machine-native transactions: the agent calls the interface, obtains the quote, signs the payment, and receives the data, all within a single HTTP request, settled in stablecoins, with a single transaction cost of only a fraction of a cent in US dollars. ACP (OpenAI/Stripe), AP2 (Google/PayPal), and Visa's TAP take a different approach, modifying existing bank card payment infrastructure to adapt to agent scenarios. These solutions are more suitable for high-value transactions; compared to settlement speed and cost, buyer protection and merchant acceptance coverage are more important.

      Stablecoins and Settlement

      Smart agents need currencies that are programmable, fast, low-cost, and global. Stablecoins fully meet these requirements, making them a natural choice for x402 and MPP transactions. Meanwhile, bank card payment channels still provide buyer protection, and merchant usage habits are mature, which remains important for high-value transactions. Underlying public blockchains (such as Base, Solana, and Tempo) raise another key question: Which chains can support the processing throughput, transaction finality, and cost structure required for large-scale agent-level transactions?

    Service Providers

    These institutions act as intermediaries between smart agents and merchants, handling complex processes such as compliance review, merchant integration, and authorization authentication. Coinbase, Stripe, and PayPal are all expanding their existing ecosystems to support agent transactions, betting that their merchant networks and compliance infrastructure will provide a competitive advantage. Other institutions, such as Sponge and Sapiom, address the cold start challenge by focusing on emerging merchants, enabling any API-based business to easily begin accepting agent payments. As the number of payment channels, protocols, and merchants continues to grow, the coordinator is expected to become a key link preventing the fragmentation of the entire system. The AI ​​Engine Layer needs little introduction; all agent interactions, inference steps, and tool calls are driven by it. However, the business models at this layer change far faster than other parts of the architecture, and the ultimate flow of value is not as clear as it appears on the surface. We focus on two main categories:

    Computing Power and Hosting

    Computing power is consumed whenever Joe's intelligent agent performs inference, invokes tools, or creates sub-agents for a task. But model inference is only one part of it. With the explosive growth of low-code/improvisational applications and agent-built services, a large number of new interfaces have emerged, all of which require hosting. As of May 2025, the number of accessible web pages has increased by 45% in just two years; and as code proxies make launching new services extremely easy, this growth rate will only accelerate further.

    The foundational large model

    The foundational large model is the "brain" of the entire system. Anthropic, OpenAI, Google, and Meta, as cutting-edge laboratories, are constantly expanding the capabilities of intelligent agents, and the cost of running these models is rapidly decreasing. At the end of 2022, the cost of running a GPT 4-level model was approximately $20 per million tokens; by early 2026, the cost of a model with equivalent performance had dropped to approximately $0.05 per million tokens, a decrease of 600 times in just over three years. Hardware upgrades, competition among manufacturers, and optimization techniques such as prompt word caching and batch processing have collectively driven down inference costs. Simultaneously, as inference logic is refined into smaller, open-source weight models with extremely low operating costs, the cost of building intelligence is also decreasing significantly. In some benchmark tests, the performance gap between open-source and closed-source weight models has narrowed to only 1.7%. This is a major boon for the machine economy. Cheaper intelligence means cheaper agents, allowing even a 24-year-old independent founder in Vermont to easily afford operating costs—further boosting transaction activity across the entire ecosystem. If large models engage in price competition like cloud service providers do today, value may ultimately concentrate in the upstream and downstream links of the model layer, rather than the model itself. Who will be the winner? By 2030, most of your digital interactions will no longer require browsers, search engines, or app stores. You simply state your needs, and intelligent agents will handle everything: finding suitable services, negotiating terms, completing payments, and delivering the final result. The internet will look completely different. This can be understood as the era of agent-centric search engine optimization. APIs will proliferate, while human-facing interfaces will diminish. In such a world, who will reap the value? Sam Ragsdale of Merit Systems has written an article comparing the current agent trading ecosystem to the early internet. He argues that the curated agent service markets (ACP, UCP, TAP) built by various platforms are following the same path as AOL in the 1990s—refined experience, closed system, but with the core limitation that all service providers must undergo manual screening and vetting. While open protocols like x402 and MPP are more rudimentary, they possess the characteristic of being permissionless: anyone can build an interface and earn revenue through proxies without the need for a business team or legal review. In the 1990s, closed-off, garden-style product experiences were superior, but the open internet held limitless possibilities. Ultimately, the open internet triumphed. The same logic is repeating itself. ACP, UCP, and TAP will interface with top AI labs, serving mainstream scenarios well, but proxies limited to pre-approved service provider directories can only complete tasks preset by the platform. Proxies that can access the entire open protocol ecosystem have far broader capabilities. It's important to understand that the most dynamic part of the internet today stems from the massive long-tail traffic from open websites brought by the HTTP protocol. We must humbly admit that we cannot yet imagine the full scope of an open proxy internet. Just as no one could predict the emergence of ride-hailing or social media in 1995, once we provide agents with the necessary tools, we cannot predict what services they will create or what services they will pay for. As we discussed earlier, the underlying big model is rapidly becoming homogenized, and value may shift to other layers of the technical architecture. Development tools, wallets, and identity infrastructure are crucial, but as standards become more unified, these areas are also likely to homogenize. Therefore, I believe that value will concentrate in three main areas: user interfaces, payments, and computing power. The user interface determines spending limits, approval processes, and trust delegation mechanisms. Platforms that can create the most personalized user experience will handle the largest transaction volume. Apple is the most underrated player in this field. Its devices are deeply integrated into people's daily lives, making user migration extremely costly. If Siri evolves into a mature proxy interaction interface, Apple won't need to build the most advanced model to control the starting point of billions of transactions. They only need to maintain the highest quality interaction interface. Google faces a more difficult transformation. Shifting from manual human browsing to intelligent proxy filtering will erode its core advertising revenue. But Google has an advantage that other companies cannot match: it has accumulated decades of personal data in search, email, calendar, maps, and documents. Furthermore, the migration costs for enterprises must be considered. Google Workspace is already embedded in millions of enterprises, with employee emails, documents, and workflows all running on Google's infrastructure. If any company can create the most personalized agent for consumers and businesses, it's undoubtedly Google. The question is whether it can monetize agency services as efficiently as it monetizes search traffic. Merit Systems is a dark horse I'm optimistic about. They are building service discovery infrastructure (AgentCash, x402 scanning, MPP scanning) for the open agent economy and developing a consumer interface (Poncho). Their core logic is: whoever controls the agent service discovery channel and intervenes in the flow of funds will occupy Google's position in the early days of the internet. This is an ambitious bet, but if open agent transactions triumph over the curated closed model, Merit will become the most advantageous aggregation layer. Currently, it is still in its early stages, just like when Google competed with AOL's closed ecosystem, which was worth an equivalent of $350 billion today. Whoever controls the flow of funds will profit from every transaction. I am most confident in the prospects of this level because its scale will grow directly in tandem with transaction volume. Stripe and Tempo have the greatest advantages in the machine-native payment field. Stripe already has a mature developer ecosystem and a vast merchant network. Tempo features streaming payments, approximately 500 millisecond transaction finality, streaming payment channels, native support for bank cards and stablecoins, USD-based gas fee payments (no token volatility risk), and server-side payment transactions, all designed specifically for the massive transaction volume of the machine economy. If MPP becomes the default machine-native payment channel, Stripe and Tempo will take a cut from every proxy transaction. Circle will grow in tandem with the expansion of the proxy economy. I firmly believe that stablecoins will become the settlement layer of the machine economy. At that time, Circle will share profits from every dollar transaction in the proxy wallet through reserve earnings. USDC is the most widely accepted stablecoin across exchanges, wallets, public chains, and payment protocols, and new developers will prioritize its use, further deepening its ecosystem integration and making it harder for competitors to enter the market. Visa will complete the adaptation. Remember when Steve Jobs topped up his credit card via Apple Pay, and the underlying transaction was automatically converted to stablecoins, without him seeing the wallet or needing to worry about the blockchain? This is the norm for the future. Consumers will continue to use familiar bank cards, with stablecoins handling the settlement. As payment channels upgrade, Visa will leverage its brand trust among consumers and merchants to solidify its position. The increase in the number of proxies means rising inference demand. The increase in impromptu development services means expanding custody demand. Regardless of which model, protocol, or interface becomes mainstream, computing power providers will benefit. AWS and Cloudflare are the two most dominant companies in this field, for similar reasons. First, they already power the majority of internet traffic. AWS holds approximately 30% of the cloud infrastructure share across 37 regions globally. Cloudflare provides security and performance services to over 20% of websites, meaning all requests from these websites pass through its network. As new proxy-oriented interfaces proliferate, developers will default to deployment platforms they are familiar with. Second, they are building the monetization infrastructure for the next generation of the internet. With the decline of advertising models and the rise of paid access models, both companies are natively supporting this transformation. Cloudflare has launched a paid crawling service, allowing any website on its network to charge its AI crawler via x402 redirects (Stack Overflow is already using this). AWS is a founding member of the x402 Foundation and has released an open-source serverless x402 reference architecture. Any service running on either of these platforms can easily enable native proxy monetization. I am pessimistic about companies like Worldcoin, whose systems require human verification for every interaction. This extreme assumption presupposes that people care whether online interactions are with people or proxies, but we've long been accustomed to this. In my view, a more likely future is that the vast majority of web traffic will be filtered based on small payments, not human identity credentials. Paid access will be more practical than "proving you're human." Identity systems are only important in some high-risk interactions, but in most proxy transactions, (small) payments themselves are credentials of trust. Conclusion When Joe wakes up, he won't be thinking about payment channels or proxy identity protocols. He'll just glance at his phone and see that the proxy has completed the transaction, scheduled a meeting, and found a cheaper server. All the technical architecture layers discussed in this article are perfectly abstracted, so he doesn't need to worry about them at all. We are still moving towards this future. **Relevant protocols are online but lack widespread adoption, the supply side is growing but still weak, service discovery issues remain unresolved, and the identity layer is severely fragmented.** Most transactions currently are just developer tests, not real proxy transactions. But the ecosystem is maturing faster than data metrics show. Those who are pessimistic about early infrastructure today only focus on the downward curve; what I'm thinking about is what the picture will look like when everyone has one or a group of proxies with real economic capabilities. If you haven't taken action yet, it's time to transition to a proxy economy model.

data.title: Powell: If inflation expectations get out of control, the Fed's patience will reach its peak.
data.description:
Author: Xiao Yanyan, Jinshi Data

Federal Reserve Chairman Jerome Powell said on Monday that the Fed prefers to keep interest rates unchanged and take a "penetrating look" approach to the energy shock triggered by the war with Iran, but warned that if rising prices begin to change public expectations of long-term inflation, the Fed may not be able to continue to stand idly by.

Speaking to students at Harvard University, Powell explained the textbook reasons for "remaining patient": energy shocks are usually short-lived, and monetary policy transmission is too slow to offset such shocks in a timely manner. However, he also raised a key but easily overlooked additional condition—inflation has been consistently above target for the past five years, making it difficult to simply assume that the public will be "indifferent" to a new round of price increases.

He said, "If similar supply shocks continue to occur, businesses, price setters, and households may gradually develop long-term expectations of higher inflation. Given that, why wouldn't they think that way?" The Federal Reserve faces a dilemma: energy shocks often have a dual effect—on the one hand, they push up prices, and on the other hand, they drag down economic growth by squeezing household budgets and raising business costs. This forces policymakers to weigh whether to prioritize curbing inflation or supporting the economy, while being keenly aware that standard tools for solving one problem often exacerbate the other. Powell appears particularly cautious about how the Federal Reserve will answer this dilemma. He stated, "We may eventually face the question of how to deal with it, but we haven't really reached that point yet because we don't yet know exactly how these economic impacts will manifest." Prior to Powell's speech, U.S. Treasury prices had already risen as investors began to withdraw their earlier bets that rising energy costs might prompt the Federal Reserve to raise interest rates again. Rising bond prices mean lower yields. Powell's remarks did not change this market trend. This appearance comes at a particular time for the Federal Reserve and for Powell personally. His term as chairman expires on May 15, and the Senate has not yet scheduled a confirmation hearing for Kevin Warsh, a former Federal Reserve governor nominated by President Trump in January to succeed Powell. Republican Senator Thom Tillis from North Carolina has indicated he will block Warsh's confirmation process until the Justice Department's investigation into Powell concludes. Earlier this month, Powell stated that he would continue as "interim chairman" if a successor had not yet been approved by then, and emphasized that he would not leave the Federal Reserve Board of Governors until the investigation was completed. At its policy meeting on March 18, the Fed voted 11-1 to maintain interest rates, keeping the target range for the federal funds rate at 3.5% to 3.75%. Stephen Miran, a governor appointed by Trump, was the only dissenting vote in favor of a rate cut. After the meeting, Powell poured cold water on the interest rate path projections submitted by his colleagues. These projections had suggested that the Fed might cut rates later this year, but Powell emphasized that these paths heavily relied on the premise that inflation needed to make progress back toward the Fed's target, progress of which had been very limited since last summer. Meanwhile, a new energy shock triggered by the Iran war has further complicated central bank decision-making. The conflict has disrupted shipping in the Strait of Hormuz and could again disrupt global supply chains. Prior to the geopolitical shock, the Fed's preferred core inflation gauge had already been trending upward this winter. Over the past two weeks, several of Powell's colleagues have further reinforced a signal that the era of "moderate rate cuts" is over—previous rate cuts were seen more as a simple reversal of earlier tightening policies. Now, they suggest the Fed is more likely to keep rates at current levels until there is a significant deterioration in the labor market or a significant decline in inflation. However, a sharp rise in energy prices could make the latter condition difficult to achieve in the short term. Nick Timiraos, a Fed mouthpiece, writes: Taken together, this shift in policy stance means the threshold for rate cuts is significantly higher than it was a few months ago—which could also make it more difficult for Warsh, once confirmed, to push through rate cuts as Trump expects. When asked by a student about the possibility of Warsh joining the Federal Reserve and pushing for interest rate cuts in the current environment, Powell avoided the question. He stated, "I'm not going to answer that question."

data.title: AI-generated prompts will disappear.
data.description:
Author: Zhang Feng

I. Cue Words: The "Icebreaker" for Human-AI Dialogue

In the past two years, with generative artificial intelligence sweeping the globe, "cue words" have transformed from an obscure technical term into an essential skill in the workplace. The market is flooded with tutorials like "Cue Word Engineering from Beginner to Expert," and social media is rife with posts claiming "Learn these ten cue words to double your AI output." People earnestly discuss techniques such as role-playing, step-by-step thinking, thought chains, and few-shot learning, as if mastering a set of exquisite cue word incantations could summon the profound power of AI.

However, what exactly are cue words?

Essentially, cue words are a kind of "translation medium" between humans and large language models. Humans describe their intentions to AI in natural language, and AI then translates these words into a search of the potential space, sampling of probability distributions, and ultimately generating a response.

The reason prompts exist is that current human-computer interaction is still in its early stages of "you ask, I answer"—AI cannot read minds, predict, or proactively ask follow-up questions; it can only passively wait for input and then mechanically complete an output. The role of prompts is to "frame" and "activate." They define the boundaries of the task, the format of the output, and the style of the response; they activate specific knowledge areas and ability modules learned by the model during the pre-training phase. A good prompt can precisely awaken a model with hundreds of billions of parameters from its "sleeping" state, like an experienced craftsman being handed the right tools. In this sense, prompts are the reins for humanity to steer AI at this stage, the icebreaker we must use when conversing with silicon-based intelligence. But the mission of an icebreaker is never to sail forever. II. Transitional Products: The Fate of Prompt Words Any form of technological interaction that requires users to learn an "intermediary language" to communicate with the system is inherently transitional. Consider the command line in the DOS era—users had to remember cumbersome instructions and parameters to make the computer work. After the advent of graphical interfaces, the command line was relegated to a secondary role. Consider also the early touchscreens that required styluses, yet Steve Jobs famously said, "God gave us ten styluses"—thus, finger interaction became mainstream. Prompt words occupy a similar transitional position. There are three reasons why prompt words are destined to disappear. First, the essence of prompt words is to "shift the cognitive burden onto the user." Users need to think about how to express themselves in a way that the AI ​​can understand, repeatedly adjust their wording, and master skills such as "role-playing" and "step-by-step reasoning." This is inherently unreasonable—like going to a restaurant where the chef requires you to first learn how to describe "Maillard reaction," "degree of caramelization," and "oil emulsification state" before you can order. A truly intelligent system should proactively adapt to humans, not force humans to adapt to the system. Second, the evolution of large-scale models is diminishing the necessity of prompt words. Early versions of GPT-3 were quite "clumsy," requiring carefully designed prompts to output useful content. However, GPT-4 has demonstrated powerful command-following and intent-understanding capabilities, responding appropriately even to the most colloquial expressions. As the model evolves to GPT-5 and higher versions, it will have increasingly stronger tolerance and completion capabilities for ambiguous, incomplete, and even contradictory human expressions. When the model is "smart" enough, prompts will no longer need to be "engineered" and can return to the most natural everyday expressions. Third, the interaction paradigm is shifting from "single-turn question-and-answer" to "multi-turn collaboration." Prompts are essentially a product of a single-turn interaction—the user packages their needs into a text message at once, and the AI ​​returns the result all at once. But truly valuable work is never a one-off event. Writing requires repeated revisions, programming requires gradual debugging, and research requires continuous in-depth exploration. Future AI interaction will be a continuous dialogue and iterative co-creation, rather than a mechanical back-and-forth of "one prompt, one answer." This brings us to an emerging form of AI interaction—OpenClaw. As an open-source AI agent framework, OpenClaw's core features are "persistent memory" and "environmental awareness." It no longer treats each conversation as an isolated event, but rather gives AI the ability to remember across conversations, perceive the current working environment (files, code, browser tabs, etc.), and proactively advance tasks based on this. In a workflow built with OpenClaw, you no longer need to repeatedly explain "who I am," "what the project background is," or "what step we've reached"—the AI ​​has "remembered" everything. In this model, "prompt words" begin to disintegrate into fragmented, natural discourse embedded in continuous interaction, rather than an independent, meticulously constructed input unit. III. The Future of AI: Both Teacher and Assistant When prompts disappear, in what form will AI exist? The answer is: AI will become both a teacher and an assistant to humanity. These two roles may seem contradictory, but they are actually unified at the same core—AI will evolve from a "passive tool" to an "active collaborator." As a teacher, AI will assume the function of "cognitive enhancement." It won't simply provide answers, but will guide human thinking. When you encounter difficulties while coding, it won't simply present a code snippet, but will ask: "What is the core problem you hope to solve? What solutions have you considered? What are the trade-offs for each solution?" Like Socrates, it will use questions to help you clarify your thinking. When you learn new knowledge, it will construct a personalized learning path based on your existing knowledge level and learning preferences, arrange reviews when you are about to forget, and change the angle of explanation when you encounter bottlenecks. It knows where you are weak and where you are strong; it understands your cognitive boundaries better than you do yourself. As an assistant, AI will assume the function of "enhancing execution." It will no longer require you to issue instructions one by one, but will be able to understand your long-term goals and proactively break them down into executable task sequences. OpenClaw has already demonstrated this possibility—it can autonomously browse web pages, manipulate files, call APIs, and send messages, completing a series of complex operations like a reliable intern, provided it is authorized. More importantly, when it encounters uncertain situations, it will proactively consult you instead of acting on its own. This "proactive execution + timely consultation" model is precisely the characteristic of an ideal assistant. Rotifer's exploration points to another dimension—continuously evolving AI. Rotifer is an open-source project that emphasizes "long-term memory" and "autonomous learning," allowing AI to continuously accumulate experience and optimize strategies through long-term interaction with users. The longer you use it, the better it understands your work habits, your thinking patterns, and your value preferences. It's not a "general model" that starts from scratch each time, but rather gradually grows into a "personalized model" tailored to your individual needs. This continuous evolutionary characteristic allows AI's role as a teacher and assistant to deepen continuously, rather than remaining superficial. Imagine this scenario: You're an independent developer working on a new project. You wake up in the morning, and your AI assistant (based on OpenClaw's persistent memory and Rotifer's continuous learning) has already compiled a task list for the day based on your code repository updates, calendar schedule, and chat history. It discovered you were stuck on a certain module yesterday, so while you rested last night, it studied relevant technical documentation and community discussions, prepared three solutions, and included an analysis of the pros and cons of each solution along with estimated workload. You sip your coffee, look at its report, and casually remark, "I think solution two is more suitable, but we need to optimize the performance a bit more." It immediately understands your intention, starts implementing it, and reports its progress to you after completing each subtask. It's not just your assistant; it's subtly teaching you better architectural thinking—because you discover that the design patterns implicit in its proposed solutions are exactly what you've always wanted to learn but haven't had time to study in depth. IV. Humanity's Task: Returning to the Expression of Needs When AI takes on the complex reasoning of "how to do it" and the task breakdown of "what to do," humanity's core task will return to a more fundamental position—expressing needs. This sounds simple, even somewhat ironic. We've become accustomed to precisely directing AI with prompts, yet now we say humans only need to express "needs"? But please carefully distinguish: expressing needs and writing prompts are fundamentally different. Writing prompts is like learning a "machine's grammar." You need to know what kind of wording will elicit what kind of output; you need to master skills like "thought chains" and "role-playing"; you need to repeatedly debug parameters and formatting. This is a process of "humans adapting to machines." Expressing needs, on the other hand, is a return to "human grammar." You can express your goals, your constraints, and your preferences in the most natural way. You can say, "I want to create an app similar to Xiaohongshu, but aimed at gardening enthusiasts, with core functions of plant identification and maintenance records. With a limited budget, I hope to use the lightest technology stack to launch an MVP within two months." This statement is full of ambiguity—"similar," "lightweight," and "MVP" don't have precise definitions, but a sufficiently intelligent AI will proactively ask questions to clarify, provide options for you to choose from, and automatically execute after you make a decision. Expressing needs is essentially the ability to "define a problem," not the ability to "describe a solution." In traditional software development, product managers define the problem, while engineers design and implement solutions. In future AI collaborations, everyone will become a "product manager"—you only need to clearly define what you want, why you want it, and what constraints you have; AI will handle the design and implementation. This doesn't mean humans become lazy or degenerate; quite the opposite, it liberates us from the tedious details of "how to implement," allowing us to focus on more creative work—defining valuable problems. This is why projects like OpenClaw and Rotifer are so important. They are building the infrastructure for this "needs expression → task breakdown → autonomous execution" process. OpenClaw's environmental awareness allows AI to understand your current context without you having to repeatedly explain the background; Rotifer's long-term memory allows AI to accumulate its understanding of you without you having to reintroduce yourself each time. When these two are combined, AI can automatically fill in the implicit information you didn't say when you express a vague need—because based on its understanding of you, it already knows how you would choose. More importantly, expressing needs is a skill that can be learned and improved. An excellent "needs communicator" can clearly define the boundaries of a problem, distinguish between core needs and secondary preferences, and anticipate the chain reactions that a decision might bring. These abilities are precisely the core advantages that distinguish humans from AI—we possess real physical experience, emotions and values, and the ability to judge "what is good" and "what is meaningful." AI can help us with calculations, execution, and optimization, but the question of "what is worth doing" will always belong to humanity. V. Farewell to Spells, Welcome Symbiosis The demise of prompt words is not a decline in AI capabilities, but rather a sign of their maturation. Just as we no longer need to remember DOS commands to use a computer, or learn stylus gestures to operate a mobile phone, we will eventually no longer need to learn "prompt word engineering" to converse with AI. When OpenClaw enables AI to possess persistent environmental awareness, and when Rotifer enables AI to continuously evolve, when these two forces converge, AI will transform from a "tool that obeys commands" into a "partner that understands intentions." It will be your teacher, illuminating the path to understanding when you are lost; it will be your assistant, sharing the burden of complex execution when you are busy. And you, as a human, only need to do what you do best—perceive the world, form judgments, and express your needs. Prompt words are our first teachers in the AI ​​era, teaching us how to communicate with silicon-based intelligence. But the mission of these first teachers is to enable students to ultimately surpass themselves. When prompts disappear, we won't miss them, just as we won't miss the command-line instructions we once learned. We will usher in a more natural and deeper human-machine relationship—not humans giving instructions to machines, but humans and machines co-creating. That will be an era where "spells" are no longer needed.

data.title: DOGE 2.0: Debt, Oil, Growth, Employment, and the Reasons for the Birth of Bitcoin
data.description:
Author: Jordi Visser, Senior Analyst on Wall Street; Translated by: Shaw Jinse Finance

Last year, when the Department of Government Efficiency (DOGE) was launched, it was touted as the ultimate solution to the problem of bloated government. However, the initiative quickly failed, leaving behind only questionable so-called "savings" and an unchanged fiscal deficit. A year later, these four letters have reappeared, defining our current reality. Only this time, DOGE stands for Debt, Oil, Growth, and Employment. These four dimensions constitute the structural dilemma facing the Federal Reserve, and it is precisely in the process of addressing this dilemma, coupled with the rise of intelligent agents (AI agents), that Bitcoin may become the most decisive core narrative in this new crisis. The irony is obvious. Washington attempted to package DOGE as an efficiency-enhancing reform, but the market now faces a much larger and more difficult-to-fix problem. Oil prices have surged as the conflict involving Iran disrupts energy transport through the Strait of Hormuz. Investors initially hoped for a quick de-escalation, but it is now clear that whenever the strait reopens, it will be a far-reaching and significant issue. Global energy supplies have been widely impacted, and inflation is bound to rise in the coming months. Meanwhile, even before the recent surge in oil prices, import price pressures were already evident; and the surge in demand driven by artificial intelligence has significantly pushed up memory chip prices, putting pressure on the supply chains of personal computers, smartphones, automobiles, and other electronic products. This is precisely the danger of the current situation. Inflation may resurface, but its causes are difficult for the Federal Reserve to address easily. At the same time, the pressure on the cost of living remains a major political issue. Raising interest rates cannot reopen the Strait of Hormuz, cannot magically increase DRAM production capacity, nor can it suddenly reduce the costs of semiconductors, memory chips, and other hardware—costs that are being passed on to sectors such as automobiles and computers. These supply-side and geopolitical shocks are impacting an economy already experiencing weakening growth momentum. And this is precisely the significance of the true D.O.G.E. analytical framework.

  • Debt is a structural constraint;

  • Oil is a source of inflationary shocks;

  • Growth will slow due to inflation and a worsening credit cycle;

  • Employment is already weak, and the Federal Reserve may soon have to prioritize its dual mandate of employment.

First, let's look at debt. It is debt that makes this cycle so different from the oil-driven inflation of the 1970s. In 1970, total U.S. federal debt was about 35.5% of GDP, which fell to 31.6% in 1979.

First, let's look at debt.

It is debt that makes this cycle so different from the oil-driven inflation of the 1970s. In 1970, total U.S. federal debt was about 35.5% of GDP, which fell to 31.6% in 1979.

Today, comparable data from the Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis (FRED) shows this ratio has reached 122.5%. Even before the global financial crisis, this ratio was far lower than it is now. This means that the United States is facing a potential second wave of inflation, with its debt burden approximately four times that of the late 1970s. This alone fundamentally alters the limits of pain the entire financial system can withstand. This is crucial because investors often draw parallels to the 1970s. On the surface, the two are indeed similar: the oil shock, inflationary pressures, and central banks facing renewed challenges after believing they have succeeded. But the state of the U.S. balance sheet today is vastly different. In the 1970s, the Federal Reserve could combat inflation with a much lighter fiscal structure; today, every hundred percentage point increase in interest rate pressure impacts economies more sensitive to borrowing costs, the Treasury market, and the federal budget. In other words, this is not a simple replay of the 1970s, but a 1970s-style predicament under a highly leveraged system. This constraint is also reflected in asset prices. The Federal Reserve is no longer facing the cheaply valued, diversified financial system of the 1970s. The current ratio of the total market capitalization of the US stock market to GDP exceeds 200%, while this figure was extremely low in the late 1970s: approximately 42% in 1975 and only 38% in 1979. The US economy is now highly financialized. This means that if the Federal Reserve is determined to suppress inflation by raising interest rates today, it is not only tightening policy against a backdrop of a weakening job market and high fiscal debt, but also implementing contraction in a market where the asset size relative to the economic size is far greater than in the 1970s. The higher the ratio of stock market capitalization to GDP, the more difficult it is for the Federal Reserve to withstand the asset deflation necessary to truly combat inflation. The labor market is another key difference. When the Federal Reserve suppressed post-pandemic inflation in 2022, US job growth was strong, wage growth was high, and policymakers had ample room to prioritize addressing inflation. The employment environment today is completely different. The February 2026 jobs report showed a decrease of 92,000 nonfarm jobs and an unemployment rate rising to 4.4%, with negligible net job growth in 2025. The unemployment rate bottomed out at 3.4% in 2023. The employment situation is even weaker, except for non-cyclical sectors like healthcare. This is by no means a booming job market, but rather a persistently weakening one. Wage growth has been declining steadily since its peak in 2023, from 6.4% to 4%. This wage situation is simply insufficient to support deliberately damaging the job market in response to the oil price shock. Jerome Powell has almost pinpointed this predicament. At a press conference on March 18, he stated that the Federal Reserve will continue to focus on its dual mandate, pointing to persistently weak job growth and acknowledging that rising energy prices may push up inflation in the short term. He also reiterated the central bank's consistent stance: "As long as inflation expectations remain stable, policymakers will generally choose to 'ignore' energy price shocks." This statement is significant, indicating that the Federal Reserve is signaling to the market that not all inflation is the same, nor does all inflation require the same policy response. Other Fed officials are also articulating the same dilemma. Vice Chairman Philip Jefferson stated that persistently high energy prices could simultaneously exacerbate inflation and curb spending, making the Fed's dual mandate even more challenging. Reuters commented that the Fed is caught in a dilemma between weak employment and high inflation. All of this coincides with a leadership transition: Powell's term as chairman will end on May 15, 2026, and Kevin Warsh has been nominated to succeed him, while President Trump continues to publicly call for immediate interest rate cuts. This will only further exacerbate the predicament. The new chairman may soon face a weakening job market, rising inflationary pressures, and public political pressure demanding looser monetary policy. So what will happen next? The Federal Reserve is unlikely to confront this round of inflation with the same ruthlessness as it did in the last round. This doesn't mean it will let inflation run rampant, but rather that it will differentiate between inflation driven by excess domestic demand and inflation caused by oil, war, tariffs, and hardware bottlenecks. If unemployment rises and hiring remains weak, the Fed will be forced to tilt towards its mandated employment goal. It may issue hawkish statements to maintain credibility, but the core logic suggests that the Fed is willing to at least partially ignore soaring inflation as long as the economy is weak enough. High debt further reinforces this tendency. The higher a country's leverage ratio, the lower its tolerance for prolonged substantial tightening. When a central bank is too burdened by debt to bear the pain of genuine economic discipline, the market will instinctively seek an asset whose supply cannot be easily expanded in preparation for the next round of bailout-style quantitative easing. This is precisely where the value of Bitcoin lies.
